lessons.title: MEETINGS, PRESENTATIONS & PROFESSIONAL WORKPLACE ETIQUETTE
lessons.introduction: Every day at BLKSM you will attend meetings, present ideas, brief suppliers, facilitate project discussions and represent the company in front of clients. Technical knowledge alone is not enough. Your ability to communicate confidently, behave professionally and conduct yourself appropriately in different workplace situations directly influences client confidence and the reputation of BLKSM. Professional etiquette is about making every interaction efficient, respectful and productive. This lesson provides practical standards for conducting meetings, delivering presentations and maintaining professional workplace behaviour.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate professional behaviour during client interactions.
lessons.leadershipThought: People often judge your professionalism before they evaluate your expertise." 1. What Is Professional Etiquette? Professional etiquette is the standard of behaviour expected in a business environment. It includes: - Respect. - Courtesy. - Punctuality. - Professional appearance. - Appropriate communication. - Accountability. - Emotional maturity. Professional etiquette creates trust. Clients feel more confident working with professionals who behave consistently and respectfully. 2. First Impressions Matter Research consistently shows that people form initial impressions within the first few moments of meeting someone. At BLKSM, first impressions are created through: - Personal appearance. - Greeting others. - Body language. - Tone of voice. - Eye contact. - Confidence. - Preparation. Every interaction represents the BLKSM brand. The BLKSM Standard Whenever you meet someone for the first time: - Smile naturally. - Stand confidently. - Introduce yourself clearly. - Offer a professional handshake where culturally appropriate. - Learn and use the person's name. - Thank them for their time. Simple professional behaviour creates lasting confidence. 3. Meeting Etiquette Meetings are designed to make decisions—not to waste time. Every participant has a responsibility to contribute professionally. Before the Meeting Prepare by: - Reading the agenda. - Reviewing supporting documents. - Completing assigned tasks. - Preparing questions. - Arriving early. Preparation demonstrates respect. During the Meeting Professional participants: - Arrive on time. - Listen actively. - Avoid interrupting. - Stay focused. - Contribute constructively. - Respect differing opinions. - Keep discussions relevant. Avoid checking your phone unless it is directly related to the meeting. After the Meeting Immediately after the meeting: - Review action items. - Confirm responsibilities. - Update project plans. - Complete assigned tasks. - Ask for clarification if required. Meetings create commitments. Professionalism means honouring those commitments. 4. Running Effective Meetings Many BLKSM employees will eventually facilitate meetings. An effective meeting should have: A Clear Objective Everyone should understand why the meeting exists. An Agenda Share the agenda before the meeting. Time Management Respect the scheduled duration. Participation Encourage discussion while keeping conversations focused. Decisions Every meeting should produce decisions. Action Items Every action should have: - An owner. - A deadline. - A measurable outcome. Without actions, meetings become conversations instead of progress. 5. Presentation Skills Presentations are common within BLKSM. Examples include: - Client proposals. - Discovery workshops. - Internal project briefings. - Supplier meetings. - Sponsorship presentations. - Post-event reports. Great presentations educate, persuade and inspire confidence. Preparing a Presentation Ask yourself: - Who is my audience? - What do they need? - What decisions should they make? - What is the key message? Do not prepare slides before understanding your audience. Presentation Structure Opening Introduce yourself. Explain the objective. Outline the agenda. Main Content Present information logically. Support recommendations with evidence. Use examples. Avoid reading directly from slides. Closing Summarise key messages. Confirm next steps. Invite questions. 6. Body Language Your body communicates continuously. Professional body language includes: - Good posture. - Eye contact. - Open gestures. - Calm movements. - Confident standing position. - Appropriate facial expressions. Avoid: - Folding arms. - Looking at the floor. - Fidgeting. - Crossing your arms defensively. - Speaking too quietly. Confidence is communicated physically as well as verbally. 7. Workplace Behaviour Professional workplaces require mutual respect. Examples include: Respect Colleagues Treat everyone professionally regardless of position. Respect Time Arrive punctually. Meet deadlines. Respond promptly. Respect Shared Spaces Keep meeting rooms tidy. Return equipment. Clean your workspace. Respect company property. Respect Confidentiality Never discuss confidential client or company information in public areas. 8. Receiving Feedback Professionally Feedback is part of professional growth. When receiving feedback: - Listen completely. - Avoid becoming defensive. - Ask clarifying questions. - Thank the person. - Develop an improvement plan. Feedback is an opportunity—not criticism. 9. Giving Feedback Professionally Good feedback is: - Specific. - Respectful. - Timely. - Behaviour-focused. - Solution-oriented. Avoid attacking the individual. Discuss behaviours rather than personalities. Example: Instead of: "You're disorganised." Say: "The supplier schedule was submitted after the agreed deadline. Let's discuss how we can improve the planning process." Professional feedback encourages improvement. 10. Professional Appearance Your appearance communicates professionalism. Employees should: - Wear appropriate attire for the role and event. - Maintain good personal grooming. - Wear company-branded clothing where required. - Display identification when appropriate. - Ensure clothing is clean and presentable. Professional appearance should always align with the event environment and client expectations. 11. Managing Difficult Conversations Not every conversation is easy. Professional event managers remain calm. When handling difficult discussions: - Listen first. - Understand the concern. - Acknowledge the issue. - Focus on facts. - Offer realistic solutions. - Confirm agreed actions. Never argue with clients or colleagues. Professionalism is often demonstrated most clearly during difficult situations. The BLKSM Way At BLKSM we believe: Professionalism is demonstrated through behaviour, not job titles. Every meeting. Every presentation. Every conversation. Every interaction. These moments build the reputation of our organisation. Clients trust professionals who are prepared, respectful and confident. Case Study The Client Presentation Two Account Managers present proposals to different clients. Account Manager A - Arrives five minutes late. - Has not tested the presentation. - Reads directly from the slides. - Cannot answer several client questions. Account Manager B - Arrives early. - Tests all equipment. - Welcomes attendees personally. - Maintains eye contact. - Explains recommendations confidently. - Invites discussion. - Summarises agreed actions before leaving. Both proposals contain similar ideas. The client awards the project to Account Manager B. The deciding factor was confidence and professionalism. Common Mistakes Avoid: - Arriving late. - Speaking over others. - Using mobile phones during meetings. - Reading slides word for word. - Ignoring questions. - Becoming defensive. - Failing to prepare. - Ending meetings without action items. Professional Tips Before every client meeting ask yourself: - Am I prepared? - Do I understand the client's objectives? - Have I anticipated likely questions? - Do I have all supporting documents? - What decision do I want the client to make? Preparation creates confidence.

lessons.reflection: Practical Exercise: Client Meeting Simulation You have been appointed as the Account Executive supporting an Account Manager during a Discovery Meeting with a new client. Prepare the following: Part 1 – Meeting Preparation - Objectives of the meeting. - Information you need before the meeting. - Documents to prepare. - Questions to ask the client. Part 2 – Presentation Plan Develop a 10-minute presentation outline covering: - Introduction. - Understanding the client's objectives. - Overview of BLKSM. - Proposed approach. - Next steps. Part 3 – Meeting Outcomes Prepare: - Meeting minutes. - Action register. - Follow-up email to the client. - Internal project update for the BLKSM team. Submission Requirements - Length: 2,000–2,500 words. - Assessment Focus: Professional communication, meeting management, presentation planning and workplace etiquette.

lessons.title: CONFLICT RESOLUTION, DIFFICULT CONVERSATIONS & EMOTIONAL INTELLIGENCE
lessons.introduction: Conflict is inevitable in event management. Deadlines change. Suppliers make mistakes. Clients change their minds. Budgets are reduced. Equipment fails. Team members disagree. The presence of conflict is not a sign of failure. The way conflict is managed determines whether it becomes a problem or an opportunity to strengthen relationships. Professional event managers remain calm, objective and solution-focused. This lesson introduces practical techniques for managing conflict, handling difficult conversations and applying emotional intelligence to maintain professionalism under pressure.
learningOutcomes: Understand the causes of workplace conflict.
Apply emotional intelligence during difficult situations.
lessons.leadershipThought: Pressure does not build character. It reveals character." 1. Understanding Conflict Conflict occurs whenever people have different: - Expectations. - Priorities. - Opinions. - Objectives. - Communication styles. - Working methods. Conflict itself is not negative. Poorly managed conflict is. Healthy conflict can improve: - Decision-making. - Innovation. - Teamwork. - Communication. - Problem-solving. Professional organisations encourage respectful discussion while preventing destructive behaviour. 2. Why Conflict Happens in Events Events operate under pressure. Many moving parts must work together simultaneously. Common causes include: Unclear Communication People misunderstand instructions. Time Pressure Deadlines increase stress. Budget Constraints Resources become limited. Changing Client Requirements Clients may request changes late in the project. Supplier Challenges Late deliveries. Equipment failures. Quality issues. Different Personalities People solve problems differently. Professional teams respect these differences. 3. Emotional Intelligence Emotional Intelligence (EQ) is the ability to understand and manage emotions—both your own and those of others. At BLKSM, emotional intelligence is considered a core professional competency. The Five Components of Emotional Intelligence Self-Awareness Recognising your own emotions. Ask yourself: - Why am I feeling frustrated? - Is my reaction appropriate? Professional behaviour begins with self-awareness. Self-Regulation Managing emotional responses. Professionals pause before reacting. They choose their response rather than reacting impulsively. Motivation Remaining focused on achieving the objective. Professional event managers remain committed even during difficult situations. Empathy Understanding another person's perspective. Empathy does not mean agreeing. It means understanding. Clients, suppliers and colleagues all want to feel heard. Relationship Management Building positive, professional relationships. Strong relationships make difficult conversations easier. 4. Responding Instead of Reacting There is a significant difference between reacting and responding. Reaction Emotional. Immediate. Often defensive. May damage relationships. Response Thoughtful. Professional. Objective. Focused on solutions. Professional event managers respond. They do not react. Example A client sends an angry email. Reaction: Reply immediately while frustrated. Response: Read carefully. Understand the concern. Gather facts. Discuss internally if necessary. Reply professionally with a solution. 5. Managing Difficult Conversations Difficult conversations become easier when following a structured approach. Step 1 – Prepare Before the conversation: - Gather facts. - Understand the issue. - Identify desired outcomes. - Consider possible solutions. Preparation creates confidence. Step 2 – Listen Allow the other person to explain. Do not interrupt. Ask clarifying questions. People become more cooperative when they feel heard. Step 3 – Acknowledge Recognise their concerns. Example: "I understand why this situation is frustrating." Acknowledgement is not admission of fault. It demonstrates respect. Step 4 – Discuss Solutions Focus on: "What can we do now?" Rather than: "Whose fault is it?" Step 5 – Confirm Actions Before ending the conversation confirm: - Decisions made. - Responsibilities. - Deadlines. - Follow-up. Never leave uncertainty. 6. Handling Client Complaints Complaints are opportunities to strengthen trust. Many loyal clients became loyal because their problems were handled exceptionally well. The BLKSM Complaint Resolution Process Listen Allow the client to explain. Acknowledge Recognise their concern respectfully. Investigate Gather facts before making commitments. Resolve Offer realistic solutions. Follow Up Confirm satisfaction after resolution. Never ignore complaints. 7. Resolving Internal Conflict Disagreements between colleagues should be addressed professionally. Avoid: - Gossip. - Blame. - Public confrontation. - Emotional arguments. Instead: - Speak privately. - Focus on facts. - Respect different opinions. - Work towards shared objectives. Remember: Everyone is working towards the same client outcome. 8. Professional Language During Conflict Avoid saying: ❌ "That's not my problem." ❌ "You're wrong." ❌ "You always..." ❌ "You never..." ❌ "It's your fault." Use language such as: ✔ "Let's look at the facts." ✔ "Help me understand your concern." ✔ "Let's work through this together." ✔ "Here's what I suggest." ✔ "What outcome are we trying to achieve?" Language influences emotion. 9. Managing Pressure on Event Day Event day brings pressure. Professional behaviour includes: - Staying calm. - Speaking clearly. - Prioritising tasks. - Supporting colleagues. - Escalating issues appropriately. - Remaining client-focused. Panic spreads quickly. Calm leadership spreads confidence. 10. Professional Accountability Sometimes mistakes happen. Professional accountability means: - Admitting mistakes honestly. - Reporting issues immediately. - Taking responsibility. - Correcting the problem. - Learning from the experience. Trying to hide mistakes almost always creates larger problems. The BLKSM Way At BLKSM we believe: Protect the relationship while solving the problem. Problems are temporary. Relationships are long-term. Professionalism means solving issues without damaging trust. Our objective is never to "win" an argument. Our objective is to achieve the best outcome for the client, the project and the team. Case Study The Catering Delay Thirty minutes before guests arrive at a gala dinner, the caterer informs the Operations Manager that a delivery vehicle has broken down, delaying service by 20 minutes. The client notices unusual activity and asks what is happening. An unprofessional response would involve blaming the caterer or saying, "Everything is falling apart." The BLKSM response would be: - Remain calm. - Gather accurate information. - Inform the client honestly. - Present a solution (for example, adjusting the programme to create additional networking time while catering catches up). - Keep the client updated until the issue is resolved. - Conduct a supplier review after the event to prevent recurrence. The problem may not have been preventable. The response is entirely within our control. Common Mistakes Avoid: - Becoming defensive. - Raising your voice. - Responding emotionally. - Interrupting others. - Making assumptions. - Blaming colleagues publicly. - Ignoring complaints. - Promising solutions before understanding the facts. Professionalism is measured most clearly during challenging situations. Professional Tips When you feel stressed: Pause. Take a moment before responding. Ask yourself: - What facts do I know? - What assumptions am I making? - What outcome do I want? - What response best reflects the BLKSM Standard? A calm response often resolves situations more effectively than a quick reaction.

sections.heading: Emotional Intelligence
sections.body:
Emotional Intelligence (EQ) is the ability to understand and manage emotions—both your own and those of others.
At BLKSM, emotional intelligence is considered a core professional competency.
The Five Components of Emotional Intelligence
Self-Awareness
Recognising your own emotions.
Ask yourself:
Why am I feeling frustrated?
Is my reaction appropriate?
Professional behaviour begins with self-awareness.
Self-Regulation
Managing emotional responses.
Professionals pause before reacting.
They choose their response rather than reacting impulsively.
Motivation
Remaining focused on achieving the objective.
Professional event managers remain committed even during difficult situations.
Empathy
Understanding another person's perspective.
Empathy does not mean agreeing.
It means understanding.
Clients, suppliers and colleagues all want to feel heard.
Relationship Management
Building positive, professional relationships.
Strong relationships make difficult conversations easier.

lessons.reflection: Practical Exercise: Conflict Resolution Simulation You are the Account Executive for a major corporate conference. Two days before the event: - The client requests significant programme changes. - The venue advises that one of the meeting rooms is no longer available due to an unexpected maintenance issue. - The marketing team has already published the original agenda. - The Operations team is concerned that implementing the changes will affect setup timelines. - The client is frustrated and expects an immediate solution. Prepare a professional response that includes: Part 1 – Situation Analysis - Identify the key issues. - List all affected stakeholders. - Prioritise the immediate actions required. Part 2 – Communication Plan Prepare: - An internal communication plan. - A client communication plan. - A venue communication plan. Part 3 – Resolution Strategy Explain: - How you would facilitate collaboration between departments. - How you would manage client expectations. - How you would minimise operational disruption. - How you would protect the BLKSM relationship with the client. Part 4 – Lessons Learned After the event, recommend: - Three improvements to prevent similar situations. - Two SOP updates. - Two communication improvements. - One KPI to monitor future performance. Submission Requirements - Length: 2,000–2,500 words. - Assessment Focus: Emotional intelligence, professional communication, conflict resolution, decision-making and adherence to the BLKSM Standard.

lessons.title: TIME MANAGEMENT, PRODUCTIVITY & PERSONAL EFFECTIVENESS
lessons.introduction: In the events industry, time is one of the most valuable resources. Unlike money, time cannot be recovered. Deadlines are fixed. Venues open at specific times. Guests arrive as scheduled. Suppliers work to agreed delivery windows. A delayed task today often becomes a crisis tomorrow. Professional event managers are not necessarily the busiest people—they are the most organised. At BLKSM, productivity is not measured by how many hours you work. It is measured by how effectively you prioritise, plan, communicate and execute your responsibilities. This lesson introduces practical techniques for managing workload, prioritising tasks and maintaining personal effectiveness in a fast-paced event environment.
learningOutcomes: Understand the principles of effective time management.
Develop habits that improve professional performance.
lessons.leadershipThought: Busy people complete tasks. Productive people achieve outcomes." 1. Why Time Management Matters Every project has deadlines. Every department depends on another. If one task is delayed, the delay affects the entire project. For example: A venue layout is approved late. -> Branding production starts late. -> Printing is delayed. -> Venue setup is delayed. -> Client confidence decreases. One missed deadline can affect dozens of people. Managing time effectively protects the project. 2. Productivity vs Being Busy Being busy is not the same as being productive. Busy people: - Constantly react. - Work without priorities. - Attend unnecessary meetings. - Respond to every interruption. - Feel overwhelmed. Productive professionals: - Plan their day. - Prioritise important work. - Complete high-impact tasks first. - Communicate proactively. - Protect time for focused work. Professional productivity creates better outcomes—not simply more activity. 3. Planning Your Day At BLKSM, every day should begin with a plan. Ask yourself: - What must be completed today? - Which tasks have the greatest impact? - Which deadlines are approaching? - Who is waiting for information from me? - What risks require attention? Planning before starting work reduces stress and improves decision-making. Daily Planning Checklist ✔ Review calendar. ✔ Review project deadlines. ✔ Check outstanding actions. ✔ Respond to urgent client communication. ✔ Prioritise high-impact work. ✔ Schedule uninterrupted focus time. ✔ Review progress before finishing the day. 4. Prioritising Work Not every task is equally important. Use the Priority Matrix. Professional event managers spend most of their time on important work before it becomes urgent. Example Instead of waiting until two days before the event to review supplier contracts, review them three weeks in advance. Preparation reduces pressure. 5. Managing Multiple Projects At BLKSM you will often work on several projects simultaneously. Successful project managers avoid treating every project equally. Instead they consider: - Event dates. - Client priorities. - Budget status. - Current project phase. - Risk level. - Resource availability. Projects should be prioritised according to business impact. 6. Managing Deadlines Deadlines should never be surprises. Professional employees: - Record every deadline. - Set reminders. - Build contingency time. - Complete work before due dates. - Communicate early if delays are unavoidable. Late communication creates bigger problems than late work. The BLKSM Rule If you believe a deadline may be missed, communicate immediately. Never wait until the deadline has already passed. 7. Managing Interruptions Modern workplaces contain constant interruptions. Examples include: - Phone calls. - Emails. - WhatsApp messages. - Teams notifications. - Walk-in conversations. - Unexpected client requests. Professional employees learn to manage interruptions without ignoring important communication. Strategies include: - Scheduling focused work sessions. - Turning off unnecessary notifications. - Grouping similar tasks together. - Checking emails at planned intervals. - Protecting planning time. 8. Delegation Delegation is not avoiding work. Delegation ensures work is completed by the most appropriate person. Effective delegation includes: - Clear expectations. - Deadlines. - Required outcomes. - Follow-up. Never assume delegated work has been completed. Verify progress. 9. Avoiding Procrastination Procrastination creates unnecessary pressure. Common causes include: - Fear of making mistakes. - Unclear priorities. - Large overwhelming tasks. - Perfectionism. - Lack of planning. Break large projects into smaller tasks. Progress creates momentum. 10. Personal Organisation Professional organisation includes: - Maintaining tidy project files. - Using approved templates. - Updating project plans. - Recording meeting actions. - Managing calendars. - Tracking outstanding tasks. An organised workspace supports an organised mind. 11. Managing Energy, Not Just Time Time management also involves managing your energy. Professional performance declines when people become: - Exhausted. - Overwhelmed. - Distracted. Healthy work habits include: - Taking appropriate breaks. - Staying hydrated. - Maintaining focus. - Managing stress. - Asking for support when workloads become unrealistic. Sustainable performance is more valuable than short bursts of activity followed by burnout. 12. Personal Accountability Professional employees own their commitments. This means: - Meeting deadlines. - Following through. - Updating colleagues. - Asking for help early. - Accepting responsibility. Reliability builds trust. People quickly learn who consistently delivers. Productivity Tools Used at BLKSM To remain organised, BLKSM uses: - Project Management Software. - Shared calendars. - Task management systems. - Meeting action registers. - Standard Operating Procedures. - Event checklists. - Risk registers. - CRM systems. Technology supports productivity. Discipline creates productivity. The BLKSM Way At BLKSM we believe: Plan your work. Prioritise your work. Complete your work. Review your work. Professional employees are dependable. Clients trust people who consistently deliver what they promised. Reliability is one of our greatest competitive advantages. Case Study Three Projects, One Account Executive An Account Executive is simultaneously supporting: - A corporate conference. - A music festival. - A charity gala. Each project has different deadlines. Instead of reacting to whichever email arrives first, the Account Executive: - Reviews all project plans every morning. - Identifies critical deadlines. - Prioritises work based on business impact. - Schedules focused work sessions. - Updates the Account Manager daily. - Communicates potential risks early. All three projects remain on schedule because the work was managed proactively rather than reactively. Common Mistakes Avoid: - Starting the day without a plan. - Ignoring your calendar. - Leaving difficult tasks until later. - Missing deadlines without communicating. - Trying to do everything yourself. - Multitasking during important work. - Allowing interruptions to control your day. - Confusing activity with productivity. Professional Tips At the end of every working day ask yourself: - What did I complete today? - What remains outstanding? - What must happen tomorrow? - Which risks require attention? - Who needs an update from me? Planning tomorrow before leaving today creates a productive start the next morning.

sections.body: Explain how effective time management contributes to better client service and successful project delivery within BLKSM. (Minimum 400 words)

lessons.reflection: Practical Exercise: Personal Productivity Improvement Plan You have recently joined BLKSM as an Account Executive. During your first month, you are responsible for supporting four different projects with overlapping deadlines. Prepare a Personal Productivity Plan that includes: Part 1 – Weekly Planning Develop a weekly schedule showing: - Project priorities. - Client meetings. - Internal meetings. - Focus work periods. - Administrative time. - Follow-up activities. Part 2 – Task Prioritisation Using the Priority Matrix: Classify 20 project tasks according to: - High Importance / High Urgency. - High Importance / Low Urgency. - Low Importance / High Urgency. - Low Importance / Low Urgency. Explain why each task belongs in its category. Part 3 – Productivity Risks Identify: - Five common productivity challenges. - The impact of each challenge. - Strategies to prevent them. Part 4 – Personal Improvement Plan Create a 90-day development plan covering: - Organisation. - Communication. - Time management. - Professional growth. - Stress management. - Personal accountability. Include measurable goals and success indicators. Submission Requirements - Length: 2,000–2,500 words. - Assessment Focus: Planning, prioritisation, productivity, personal effectiveness and application of BLKSM standards.

Unlike previous modules, Module 4 focuses heavily on practical workplace skills. Learners are expected not only to understand the theory but also to demonstrate how they would communicate and behave in realistic business situations.

Professional documentation protects both BLKSM and its clients.
A client emails at 18:00 requesting major changes to the event programme scheduled for the following morning. Prepare:
questions.gradingGuidance: Should address: A professional email response.; An internal communication plan.; A list of immediate actions.; A risk assessment.; A recommendation for the Account Manager.

questions.promptText: A supplier repeatedly misses agreed deadlines, affecting project progress. Prepare a structured conversation explaining how you would:
questions.gradingGuidance: Should address: Introduce the discussion.; Explain the impact.; Listen to the supplier's perspective.; Agree on corrective actions.; Protect the long-term relationship.

questions.promptText: At 09:00 on Monday morning you receive: Describe:
questions.gradingGuidance: Should address: Three client emails.; Two supplier issues.; An urgent request from the Managing Director.; A proposal due at 15:00.; A Discovery Meeting at 11:00.; Your priority order.; Why you chose it.; Which tasks can be delegated.; How you would communicate with stakeholders.; How you would ensure nothing is missed.

questions.promptText: Professional communication is often described as the foundation of successful project management. Explain why communication is critical within BLKSM and describe how poor communication can affect clients, suppliers, colleagues and project outcomes. Minimum Response: 400 words.

Describe how emotional intelligence, workplace etiquette and effective time management work together to improve both employee performance and client satisfaction. Provide practical workplace examples. Minimum Response: 500 words. PRACTICAL WORKPLACE PORTFOLIO (20 Marks) Assignment Title Professional Communication Portfolio You have joined BLKSM as a new Account Executive. Prepare a complete communication portfolio for a fictional corporate event. Part 1 – Client Communication Write: - Welcome email. - Discovery Meeting invitation. - Project progress update. - Programme change notification. - Thank-you email after the event. Part 2 – Internal Communication Prepare: - Internal project briefing. - Meeting agenda. - Meeting minutes. - Department action register. - Event Readiness reminder. Part 3 – Supplier Communication Write: - Request for quotation (RFQ). - Supplier appointment confirmation. - Delivery confirmation request. - Supplier issue escalation email. - Supplier appreciation message after the event. Part 4 – Professional Meeting Develop: - A 30-minute meeting agenda. - Presentation outline. - Speaking notes. - Action register. - Follow-up summary. Part 5 – Personal Productivity Plan Create a one-week work plan that includes: - Daily priorities. - Calendar schedule. - High-priority tasks. - Follow-up reminders. - Personal productivity strategies. - Risk management actions. Submission Requirements - Length: 2,500–3,000 words - Assessment Weight: 20% Marking Rubric Facilitator Assessment Guidelines To pass Module 4, learners should demonstrate that they can: - Communicate professionally across multiple business channels. - Produce clear, accurate and well-structured documentation. - Facilitate and participate effectively in meetings. - Resolve workplace and client issues professionally. - Apply emotional intelligence in challenging situations. - Manage competing priorities while maintaining productivity. - Represent BLKSM professionally in all interactions. LMS Configuration - Question Bank: 150 questions - Questions per Attempt: 35 (randomly selected) - Question Order: Randomised - Answer Order: Randomised - Time Limit: 90 minutes - Passing Score: 80% - Maximum Attempts: 3 - Prerequisite: Completion of Lessons 1–5 - Unlock Rule: Module 5 unlocks automatically once the learner has passed the assessment and the practical portfolio has been approved. Module 4 Competency Achievement A learner who successfully completes Module 4 should be able to: - Communicate with clients, suppliers and colleagues in a professional and confident manner. - Produce business-standard emails, reports, meeting agendas and minutes. - Conduct themselves professionally in meetings and presentations. - Resolve conflict using emotional intelligence and solution-focused communication. - Organise their workload effectively, prioritise competing demands and consistently meet deadlines. - Represent the BLKSM brand with professionalism, confidence and integrity in every interaction. This assessment confirms that learners are ready to progress to Module 5 – Business Systems, Technology & Digital Productivity, where they will begin working with the operational systems, software and digital workflows that support BLKSM's day-to-day project delivery.

modules.title: Business Systems, Technology & Digital Productivity
modules.description: This assessment evaluates the learner's understanding of BLKSM's digital workplace, project management systems, CRM, cybersecurity, data governance, Artificial Intelligence and workflow automation. Upon successful completion, learners should be able to confidently use BLKSM's business systems while maintaining high standards of digital professionalism, security and operational excellence.

lessons.title: DIGITAL WORKPLACE & BUSINESS SYSTEMS
lessons.introduction: Modern event management is powered by technology. Successful events are no longer managed with spreadsheets, notebooks and endless email chains alone. Every project depends on digital systems that enable teams to collaborate, communicate, manage budgets, track suppliers, register attendees, monitor deadlines and report results. At BLKSM, technology is used to improve efficiency—not replace people. Every employee is expected to understand the company's digital ecosystem and use business systems consistently. Technology creates visibility. Processes create consistency. People create exceptional experiences. This lesson introduces the digital workplace and explains how business systems support every stage of the BLKSM Event Delivery Framework.
learningOutcomes: Understand the role of business systems within BLKSM.
Identify the core digital platforms used across the organisation.
Explain how information flows between systems.
Understand the importance of accurate data.
lessons.leadershipThought: Technology doesn't make great companies. Great companies use technology exceptionally well." 1. The Modern Digital Workplace A digital workplace is an environment where employees use technology to communicate, collaborate and manage work. Instead of information being stored in individual notebooks or personal computers, information is stored in structured systems that can be accessed securely by authorised team members. Benefits include: - Faster collaboration. - Better reporting. - Improved accountability. - Reduced duplication. - Easier project management. - Better client service. The objective is simple: Everyone works from the same information. 2. Why Business Systems Matter Imagine an Account Executive updates the event schedule. Marketing continues using the old version. Operations receives a different version. Suppliers receive outdated information. The result is confusion, delays and unnecessary cost. Business systems create one trusted source of information. They ensure everyone is working from the latest approved version. 3. The BLKSM Digital Ecosystem Every department uses technology to perform its work. Although individual software platforms may change over time, the functions remain consistent. The BLKSM Digital Ecosystem includes: CLIENTS -> Customer Relationship Management (CRM) -> Project Management System -> Communication Platforms -> Document Management -> Finance & Procurement -> Marketing Systems -> Reporting & Analytics Information flows between systems throughout the life of every project. 4. Customer Relationship Management (CRM) The CRM is the heart of client management. Every client interaction should be recorded. The CRM stores: - Client information. - Contact details. - Opportunities. - Discovery notes. - Quotations. - Communication history. - Project status. - Follow-up reminders. The CRM creates continuity. If an Account Manager is unavailable, another team member can immediately understand the client's history. 5. Project Management Systems Project Management Software coordinates daily work. Examples of information managed include: - Tasks. - Deadlines. - Milestones. - Dependencies. - Team assignments. - Project status. - Deliverables. - Risk registers. Every project should have one central project workspace. This prevents important information from being scattered across emails and chat messages. 6. Communication Platforms Communication systems support collaboration. Typical business communication includes: - Internal messaging. - Video meetings. - File sharing. - Team announcements. - Project discussions. Good communication platforms improve speed. However, important decisions should always be recorded in the official project records. Chat messages are not a substitute for formal project documentation. 7. Document Management Professional organisations manage thousands of documents. Every document should be: - Secure. - Organised. - Version controlled. - Easily searchable. - Accessible to authorised users. Examples include: - Client proposals. - Contracts. - Floor plans. - Budgets. - Risk registers. - Supplier agreements. - Event reports. - SOPs. Poor document management creates operational risk. 8. Marketing Systems Marketing platforms support: - Social media scheduling. - Email marketing. - Website management. - Content planning. - Campaign reporting. - Lead generation. Marketing data should be integrated into project reporting wherever possible. Understanding campaign performance allows BLKSM to make informed business decisions. 9. Finance Systems Financial systems manage: - Client invoices. - Purchase orders. - Supplier payments. - Expense tracking. - Budget monitoring. - Financial reporting. Financial accuracy is critical. Incorrect financial data affects project profitability and client confidence. 10. Data Quality Technology is only as reliable as the information entered into it. Poor data leads to: - Incorrect reports. - Missed deadlines. - Duplicate work. - Poor client communication. - Financial errors. Every employee shares responsibility for maintaining accurate information. BLKSM Data Standards Always ensure information is: ✔ Accurate. ✔ Complete. ✔ Up to date. ✔ Verified. ✔ Relevant. ✔ Secure. 11. Information Security Business information is valuable. Employees must protect: - Client information. - Commercial proposals. - Budgets. - Supplier pricing. - Staff information. - Strategic documents. Good security habits include: - Strong passwords. - Multi-factor authentication. - Locking computers when unattended. - Never sharing passwords. - Reporting suspicious activity immediately. Technology security is everyone's responsibility. 12. System Discipline Technology only works when everyone follows the same processes. Examples include: - Updating project status promptly. - Recording meeting outcomes. - Closing completed tasks. - Uploading approved documents. - Recording client communication. Incomplete systems create incomplete projects. The BLKSM Way At BLKSM we believe: If the information isn't recorded in the system, it doesn't exist. Business systems are not administrative tasks. They are operational tools that help us deliver exceptional service. Reliable systems create reliable businesses. Case Study The Missing Supplier Information An Operations Manager arrives at a venue expecting staging equipment at 08:00. The supplier arrives at 12:00. Investigation reveals: - The updated delivery schedule was sent by email. - The Project Management System was never updated. - Operations continued working from the previous schedule. The delay affects setup, rehearsals and client confidence. Had the project system been updated correctly, the issue would have been identified immediately. Technology did not fail. Process discipline failed. Common Mistakes Avoid: - Saving documents only on your computer. - Forgetting to update project status. - Working from outdated files. - Recording information in multiple locations. - Ignoring version control. - Sharing confidential documents unnecessarily. - Assuming someone else updated the system. Professional Tips Before ending every working day: Ask yourself: - Have I updated today's project activities? - Are my tasks current? - Have I uploaded today's documents? - Have I recorded important decisions? - Can another colleague understand the current project status if I am absent tomorrow? Good systems support good teamwork.

Business systems enable BLKSM to manage projects consistently, collaborate effectively and deliver exceptional client experiences.

Your manager asks you to prepare a guide explaining how information moves through the organisation. Prepare a report covering: Part 1 – Business Systems Describe the purpose of: - CRM - Project Management System - Communication Platform - Document Management System - Finance System - Marketing System Part 2 – Information Flow Explain how information moves from: - Client enquiry - Discovery - Proposal - Planning - Event Delivery - Close-Out - Reporting Identify which systems are used at each stage. Part 3 – Data Quality Explain: - Why data accuracy matters. - Common mistakes. - Risks created by poor information. - Best practices for maintaining reliable systems. Part 4 – Security Develop a Digital Security Checklist for all BLKSM employees covering: - Password management. - Multi-factor authentication. - File permissions. - Confidential information. - Device security. - Incident reporting. Submission Requirements - Length: 2,000–2,500 words. - Assessment Focus: Digital literacy, business systems, information management and operational discipline.

lessons.title: PROJECT MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS, TASK MANAGEMENT & WORKFLOW AUTOMATION
lessons.introduction: Every successful event is the result of thousands of individual tasks completed in the correct order. No professional event manager relies on memory alone. Modern project management systems provide structure, accountability and visibility throughout the project lifecycle. At BLKSM, every task, approval, deadline and responsibility should be managed through the Project Management System (PMS). This system is the operational command centre of every project. When used correctly, it allows everyone to know: - What needs to be done. - Who is responsible. - When it must be completed. - What is currently at risk. - What has already been completed. A disciplined workflow enables BLKSM to deliver complex projects consistently, even when multiple departments are involved.
learningOutcomes: Understand the purpose of a Project Management System.
lessons.leadershipThought: Projects don't fail because there is too much work. They fail because the work is unmanaged." 1. What is Project Management? Project Management is the structured planning, coordination and control of work required to achieve a defined objective within agreed: - Scope - Time - Budget - Quality standards Every BLKSM event is treated as a project. Whether it is: - A 50-person board meeting - A music festival - A conference - A product launch - A charity gala The same management principles apply. 2. The BLKSM Project Management Philosophy Every project should answer five questions: What needs to be done? Who is responsible? When must it be completed? What depends on it? How do we know it is complete? If these five questions cannot be answered, the project is not under control. 3. Understanding Tasks A project consists of hundreds or even thousands of individual tasks. Examples include: - Book venue. - Confirm catering. - Finalise branding. - Approve artwork. - Issue purchase orders. - Build registration page. - Conduct venue inspection. - Complete risk assessment. - Confirm staffing. - Print accreditation badges. Every task should include: - Task name. - Description. - Owner. - Due date. - Priority. - Status. - Dependencies. - Supporting documents. Tasks should never exist only in someone's memory. 4. Task Statuses BLKSM uses standard task statuses to provide visibility. Typical statuses include: - Not Started - Planned - In Progress - Waiting for Approval - Waiting on Client - Waiting on Supplier - Blocked - Completed - Cancelled Every employee is responsible for updating task status accurately. An outdated project system creates project risk. 5. Understanding Dependencies Many tasks cannot begin until another task has been completed. These are called dependencies. Example: Venue confirmed -> Floor plan developed -> Branding artwork produced -> Printing completed -> Venue setup -> Client walkthrough If one task is delayed, every dependent task is affected. Professional project managers identify dependencies early. 6. Milestones A milestone represents a significant achievement within a project. Examples include: - Proposal Approved - Contract Signed - Venue Confirmed - Budget Approved - Supplier Appointed - Registration Opens - Marketing Campaign Launch - Event Readiness Approved - Event Delivered - Project Closed Milestones do not represent work. They represent progress. 7. Workflow Automation Workflow Automation allows routine activities to happen automatically. Instead of relying on people to remember every step, the system performs predefined actions. Examples include: When the proposal is approved: - Create project workspace. - Assign Account Manager. - Generate project folder. - Schedule Kick-Off Meeting. - Notify Finance. - Notify Operations. Automation improves consistency while reducing manual administration. Example Workflow Proposal Approved -> Project Created Automatically -> Departments Notified -> Kick-Off Meeting Scheduled -> Project Templates Generated -> Tasks Assigned Automatically 8. Project Dashboards Project Dashboards provide a real-time overview of project health. Instead of reading hundreds of individual tasks, managers can immediately identify: - Project progress. - Upcoming deadlines. - Outstanding approvals. - Budget status. - High-risk items. - Overdue tasks. - Team workload. Dashboards support faster decision-making. 9. Workload Management Managers must ensure work is distributed fairly. The Project Management System should show: - Number of active projects. - Tasks per employee. - Hours allocated. - Upcoming deadlines. - Capacity. Overloaded employees create project risk. Balanced workloads improve performance. 10. Escalation Not every issue can be solved immediately. When tasks become blocked, the system should support escalation. Examples include: Task overdue for 48 hours -> Automatic notification to Account Manager -> 72 hours overdue -> Notification to Department Head -> 96 hours overdue -> Executive escalation Escalation prevents problems from remaining hidden. 11. Standard Project Templates Every BLKSM project begins with a standard project template. Templates improve: - Consistency. - Speed. - Quality. - Training. - Reporting. Typical templates include: - Conference - Music Festival - Product Launch - Golf Day - Awards Ceremony - Charity Event - Corporate Function - Brand Activation Templates provide a starting point. Projects can then be customised. 12. The Importance of Updating the System A Project Management System is only valuable when it reflects reality. Every employee should update: - Task completion. - Delays. - Risks. - Client approvals. - Supplier changes. - Documents. - Comments. If the system is not updated, managers cannot make informed decisions. The BLKSM Way At BLKSM we believe: If a task has no owner, no deadline and no status, it does not exist. Every task must have: ✔ Ownership ✔ Accountability ✔ Visibility ✔ Measurable progress Strong systems reduce uncertainty. Case Study Conference Planning BLKSM is managing a conference for 1,800 delegates. The Project Management System automatically creates 640 standard tasks using the Conference Template. Each department receives its responsibilities. Marketing receives campaign tasks. Operations receives venue tasks. Finance receives budget tasks. Procurement receives supplier tasks. The Account Manager monitors progress using the project dashboard. When catering confirmation becomes overdue, the system automatically alerts the Account Manager, who contacts Procurement before the issue delays production planning. The event is delivered successfully because the system identified risks before they became problems. Common Mistakes Avoid: - Creating tasks without owners. - Missing due dates. - Ignoring overdue tasks. - Creating duplicate tasks. - Updating tasks after deadlines have passed. - Managing projects through email alone. - Assuming colleagues know task status. Visibility creates accountability. Professional Tips At the end of each day: Review: - Outstanding tasks. - Upcoming deadlines. - Blocked work. - Client approvals. - Team dependencies. Ask: "If I were absent tomorrow, would another colleague understand exactly where this project stands?" If the answer is yes, your project system is working.

sections.heading: Written Reflection (not auto-graded)
sections.body: Explain how workflow automation improves project consistency and reduces operational risk within BLKSM. Your answer should include examples of automated approvals, task creation, notifications and escalation processes. (Minimum 450 words)

lessons.reflection: Practical Exercise: Designing a BLKSM Project Workflow A new client has appointed BLKSM to organise a three-day technology expo. Design a complete project workflow including: Part 1 – Project Structure Create: - Project phases. - Milestones. - Major deliverables. - Department ownership. Part 2 – Task Breakdown Develop at least 50 project tasks. For each task include: - Owner. - Priority. - Due date. - Status. - Dependency. Part 3 – Workflow Automation Recommend 10 automation rules, including: - Automatic task creation. - Client approval workflows. - Deadline reminders. - Escalation rules. - Department notifications. - Document approvals. Part 4 – Dashboard Design Design a Project Dashboard showing: - Overall completion percentage. - Tasks overdue. - Upcoming milestones. - Budget status. - Risk level. - Department workload. - Outstanding approvals. - Project health indicator. Part 5 – Process Improvements Recommend five ways BLKSM could improve project delivery through better workflow automation and system discipline. Submission Requirements - Length: 2,500–3,000 words. - Assessment Focus: Project management, workflow design, automation, accountability and operational excellence.

options.optionText: A task that relies on another task being completed first.

options.optionText: They provide real-time visibility into project health, risks and progress.

lessons.title: CRM, CLIENT DATA MANAGEMENT & BUSINESS INTELLIGENCE
lessons.introduction: Clients are the most valuable asset of any event management company. Events come and go. Projects are completed. Suppliers change. Technology evolves. But long-term client relationships create sustainable businesses. At BLKSM, every interaction with a client is recorded, tracked and managed through the Customer Relationship Management (CRM) system. The CRM is much more than a contact list. It is the memory of the organisation. It allows BLKSM to understand client history, monitor opportunities, strengthen relationships and make informed business decisions. This lesson introduces CRM principles, client data management and the role of Business Intelligence in helping BLKSM grow strategically.
learningOutcomes: Understand the purpose of a CRM.
Use Business Intelligence to improve decision-making.
lessons.leadershipThought: A company that knows its clients well will always outperform a company that simply knows its projects." 1. What is a CRM? CRM stands for Customer Relationship Management. A CRM is a business system used to manage every interaction between BLKSM and its clients. The CRM helps us answer questions such as: - Who is the client? - What projects have we completed for them? - Who are the decision-makers? - What proposals are outstanding? - When should we follow up? - What opportunities exist? - What challenges have they experienced? Without a CRM, valuable information is often lost when employees leave or projects end. 2. Why CRM Matters Imagine a client contacts BLKSM after two years. Without a CRM: - Previous conversations are lost. - Budgets cannot be found. - Event preferences are forgotten. - Contacts have changed. - Opportunities are missed. With a CRM: The Account Manager immediately sees: - Previous events. - Event budgets. - Client preferences. - Meeting history. - Proposals. - Feedback. - Contracts. - Outstanding opportunities. The client feels remembered. Professional organisations remember clients. 3. The Client Lifecycle Every client follows a journey. Prospect -> Lead -> Discovery -> Proposal -> Client -> Project Delivery -> Post-Event Review -> Repeat Business -> Strategic Partner The objective is not simply to win projects. The objective is to build lasting partnerships. 4. Client Profiles Every client profile should contain accurate and complete information. Typical CRM fields include: Company Information - Company Name - Industry - Website - Physical Address - Billing Information Primary Contacts - Name - Position - Email Address - Mobile Number - Preferred Communication Method Business Information - Annual Event Calendar - Estimated Budget - Business Objectives - Key Stakeholders - Decision Makers Project History - Previous Events - Event Types - Budgets - Feedback - Challenges - Successes Relationship Information - Last Contact Date - Next Follow-Up Date - Account Manager - Relationship Status - Client Satisfaction Rating A complete profile enables exceptional service. 5. Opportunity Management Every potential project begins as an opportunity. The CRM should track every opportunity from first enquiry to signed contract. Typical stages include: - New Enquiry - Qualified Lead - Discovery Scheduled - Proposal in Progress - Proposal Submitted - Negotiation - Won - Lost - On Hold Monitoring opportunities helps BLKSM forecast future revenue. 6. Recording Client Interactions Every meaningful interaction should be recorded. Examples include: - Discovery Meetings. - Phone calls. - Emails. - Site inspections. - Proposal presentations. - Client complaints. - Follow-up meetings. - Feedback sessions. Recording interactions creates continuity. If another team member becomes involved, they immediately understand the relationship. 7. Client Segmentation Not every client has the same needs. The CRM allows BLKSM to group clients according to different characteristics. Examples include: Industry - Corporate - Retail - Government - Education - Healthcare - Hospitality - Non-Profit Event Type - Conferences - Festivals - Awards - Product Launches - Golf Days - Brand Activations Relationship Value - Platinum - Gold - Silver - Bronze Annual Revenue - Strategic Accounts - Key Accounts - Growth Accounts - New Clients Segmentation helps prioritise business development activities. 8. Business Intelligence (BI) Business Intelligence transforms data into meaningful information. Instead of simply storing information, BI answers questions such as: - Which industries generate the highest revenue? - Which clients return every year? - Which Account Managers have the highest proposal conversion rates? - Which services are growing fastest? - Which marketing campaigns generate the most enquiries? Information supports better business decisions. 9. CRM Dashboards CRM dashboards provide management with a real-time overview of business performance. Examples include: Sales Dashboard - New leads. - Active opportunities. - Proposal value. - Revenue forecast. - Win rate. Client Dashboard - Active clients. - Client satisfaction. - Repeat business. - Follow-up reminders. - Strategic accounts. Account Manager Dashboard - Projects managed. - Outstanding proposals. - Follow-up tasks. - Client meetings. - Conversion rates. Dashboards allow leaders to identify trends quickly. 10. Data Quality A CRM is only valuable if the information is reliable. Every employee should ensure that information is: - Accurate. - Current. - Complete. - Consistent. - Verified. Poor data affects every department. Incorrect contact details may result in: - Missed meetings. - Incorrect invoices. - Delayed approvals. - Lost opportunities. 11. Confidentiality & Data Protection Client information is confidential. Employees should: - Only access information required for their role. - Never export client data without approval. - Never share client information externally. - Lock computers when unattended. - Report data breaches immediately. Professional trust depends on responsible information management. 12. Using CRM to Strengthen Relationships The CRM should never become a passive database. Instead, it should remind Account Managers to: - Schedule follow-up meetings. - Congratulate clients on business milestones. - Recommend new event ideas. - Share industry trends. - Invite clients to BLKSM events. - Conduct annual planning sessions. Great relationships require proactive communication. The BLKSM Way At BLKSM we believe: Every client should feel like our only client. The CRM helps us personalise every interaction. Remembering birthdays, previous projects, preferred venues, communication preferences and strategic objectives creates exceptional client experiences. Technology helps us remember. People build relationships. Case Study From One Conference to a Five-Year Partnership A financial services company contacts BLKSM to organise a single leadership conference. The Account Manager records detailed Discovery notes in the CRM, including the client's long-term growth plans and annual event calendar. After the event, the CRM automatically schedules a follow-up meeting three months later. During that meeting, the Account Manager proposes a leadership roadshow and an annual awards ceremony based on the client's strategic objectives. Within two years, BLKSM manages six annual events for the client. The relationship grew because the CRM encouraged proactive engagement rather than reactive selling. Common Mistakes Avoid: - Recording incomplete client information. - Forgetting follow-up dates. - Creating duplicate client profiles. - Leaving opportunities without updates. - Storing client information outside approved systems. - Sharing confidential information unnecessarily. - Treating the CRM as an administrative task instead of a business tool. Professional Tips Before closing a client interaction, ask yourself: - Have I recorded today's meeting? - Have I updated the opportunity stage? - Have I scheduled the next follow-up? - Have I uploaded any relevant documents? - Can another Account Manager immediately understand the current client relationship? If the answer is yes, your CRM is current.

lessons.reflection: Practical Exercise: Designing the BLKSM CRM You have been appointed to assist in designing BLKSM's new CRM platform. Prepare a CRM blueprint that includes: Part 1 – Client Profile Design Create a complete client profile including: - Company Information - Contact Information - Decision Makers - Event History - Budget Information - Communication Preferences - Client Rating - Relationship Status Part 2 – Opportunity Pipeline Design the opportunity workflow from: - Lead - Discovery - Proposal - Negotiation - Won/Lost - Project Handover Define the criteria required to move between each stage. Part 3 – CRM Dashboard Design a dashboard displaying: - Active Opportunities - Revenue Forecast - Proposal Conversion Rate - Client Satisfaction - Upcoming Follow-Ups - Strategic Accounts - Repeat Business Rate - Top Performing Account Managers Part 4 – Automation Rules Recommend at least 10 CRM automations, such as: - Automatic follow-up reminders. - Proposal expiry notifications. - Client birthday reminders. - Annual event reminders. - Escalation of inactive opportunities. - Welcome email sequences. - Post-event feedback requests. - Quarterly client review reminders. Part 5 – Data Governance Develop a CRM policy covering: - Data ownership. - User permissions. - Data quality standards. - Confidentiality. - Record retention. - Audit requirements. Submission Requirements - Length: 2,500–3,000 words. - Assessment Focus: CRM strategy, client management, Business Intelligence, automation and data governance.

lessons.title: CYBERSECURITY, DATA PROTECTION & DIGITAL COMPLIANCE
lessons.introduction: BLKSM manages valuable information every day. Client databases. Event budgets. Supplier contracts. Marketing strategies. Employee records. Commercial proposals. Payment information. If this information is compromised, the consequences can be severe. A cyberattack or accidental data breach can damage client trust, delay projects, create financial losses and harm the reputation of BLKSM. Cybersecurity is not the responsibility of the IT Department alone. Every employee who uses a computer, mobile phone or cloud system plays a critical role in protecting the organisation. This lesson introduces the cybersecurity principles, digital compliance standards and data protection practices expected at BLKSM.
learningOutcomes: Recognise phishing and social engineering attacks.
lessons.leadershipThought: A company's greatest cybersecurity risk is rarely its technology—it is human behaviour." 1. Why Cybersecurity Matters Modern organisations depend on digital systems. Without secure systems: - Projects stop. - Data is lost. - Clients lose confidence. - Financial losses occur. - Business operations are disrupted. Every employee contributes to digital security. Cybersecurity protects: - Clients. - Employees. - Suppliers. - Company information. - Business continuity. 2. What Is Cybersecurity? Cybersecurity is the practice of protecting: - Systems. - Networks. - Devices. - Applications. - Data. From: - Unauthorised access. - Theft. - Damage. - Disruption. - Malicious attacks. Good cybersecurity combines: - Technology. - Policies. - Processes. - Employee behaviour. 3. Common Cyber Threats Phishing Fraudulent emails or messages designed to steal passwords or sensitive information. Example: An email appears to come from the Managing Director requesting an urgent bank transfer. Always verify unusual requests. Malware Malicious software designed to damage systems or steal information. Examples include: - Viruses. - Trojans. - Spyware. - Keyloggers. Never install unauthorised software. Ransomware Attackers encrypt company files and demand payment to restore access. Ransomware can stop an entire business from operating. Regular backups reduce this risk. Social Engineering Attackers manipulate people rather than technology. Examples: - Pretending to be a supplier. - Impersonating senior management. - Requesting confidential information. - Creating false urgency. Think before you trust. Password Attacks Weak or reused passwords are among the most common causes of security breaches. 4. Password Security Every employee is responsible for protecting login credentials. Strong passwords should: - Be at least 14 characters long. - Include upper and lower-case letters. - Include numbers. - Include special characters. - Be unique for every system. Never: - Share passwords. - Write passwords on sticky notes. - Reuse passwords across systems. - Send passwords by email or WhatsApp. Use a trusted password manager where approved by BLKSM. 5. Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) MFA requires two or more methods of verification before granting access. Examples: - Password. - Authentication App. - SMS Code. - Fingerprint. - Face Recognition. MFA dramatically reduces the risk of unauthorised access. Whenever available, MFA should always be enabled. 6. Protecting Company Devices BLKSM equipment should always be used responsibly. Employees should: - Lock computers when leaving desks. - Keep laptops secure. - Report lost devices immediately. - Install approved updates. - Avoid public computers for company work. - Never disable security software. Physical security is part of cybersecurity. 7. Email Security Email remains one of the most common attack methods. Before opening an email ask: - Do I recognise the sender? - Does the email make sense? - Is the request unusual? - Are there spelling mistakes? - Is there unexpected urgency? - Am I expecting this attachment? If unsure: Do not click. Verify independently. Report the email. 8. Safe Internet Usage Employees should: - Visit trusted websites only. - Avoid downloading unauthorised software. - Never bypass company security controls. - Avoid connecting unknown USB devices. - Use secure Wi-Fi networks. Public Wi-Fi should only be used with approved secure connections. 9. Data Classification Not all information has the same level of sensitivity. BLKSM classifies information into four categories. Public Information approved for public release. Examples: - Website content. - Marketing material. - Public event announcements. Internal Information intended only for BLKSM employees. Examples: - Internal procedures. - Meeting minutes. - Internal communications. Confidential Information that could damage BLKSM or clients if disclosed. Examples: - Budgets. - Contracts. - Supplier pricing. - Project plans. - Client proposals. Restricted Highly sensitive information. Examples: - Banking details. - Employee records. - Passwords. - Security credentials. - Legal documents. Restricted information should only be accessible to authorised personnel. 10. Protecting Personal Information BLKSM must protect personal information responsibly. Examples include: - Employee information. - Client contact details. - Supplier information. - Event attendee registrations. Only collect information that is necessary. Only keep information for as long as required. Only share information with authorised individuals. 11. Digital Compliance Compliance means following legal, contractual and company requirements. Employees must comply with: - Company Information Security Policies. - Client confidentiality agreements. - Data privacy legislation. - Acceptable Use Policies. - Digital record retention standards. Compliance protects both BLKSM and its clients. 12. Responding to Security Incidents If you suspect a cybersecurity incident: Immediately: - Stop using the affected system if necessary. - Inform your manager. - Notify the IT Department. - Record what happened. - Do not attempt to hide the issue. Early reporting often prevents major incidents. Never ignore suspicious activity. The BLKSM Cyber Response Process Suspicious Activity Detected -> Stop & Secure -> Notify IT Immediately -> Notify Your Manager -> Incident Logged -> Investigation -> Resolution -> Lessons Learned 13. Your Personal Responsibility Every employee should: ✔ Protect passwords. ✔ Keep systems updated. ✔ Report suspicious activity. ✔ Follow company policies. ✔ Protect confidential information. ✔ Think before clicking. ✔ Ask when uncertain. Cybersecurity begins with everyday habits. The BLKSM Way At BLKSM we believe: Protecting client information is protecting client trust. Technology alone cannot secure a business. Professional behaviour is our strongest defence. Every employee is part of the cybersecurity team. Case Study The Fake Invoice An Accounts Executive receives an email that appears to come from a trusted supplier. The email states that the supplier has changed its banking details and requests that all future payments be made to a new account immediately. Instead of updating the banking information, the employee follows the BLKSM verification process. They contact the supplier using the phone number already stored in the CRM. The supplier confirms that no banking changes have been made. The email was a phishing attempt. Because company procedures were followed, BLKSM prevented a significant financial loss. Common Mistakes Avoid: - Clicking unknown links. - Sharing passwords. - Using personal cloud storage for company files. - Ignoring software updates. - Leaving computers unlocked. - Downloading unauthorised software. - Discussing confidential information in public places. - Assuming someone else has reported a security concern. Professional Tips Before opening any unexpected email attachment, ask: - Was I expecting this? - Does the sender normally send attachments? - Does the message create unnecessary urgency? - Have I verified the request independently? If something feels suspicious, stop and verify.

Ignoring software updates.
Leaving computers unlocked.
Downloading unauthorised software.
Discussing confidential information in public places.
Assuming someone else has reported a security concern.

sections.heading: Professional Tips
sections.body:
Before opening any unexpected email attachment, ask:
Was I expecting this?
Does the sender normally send attachments?
Does the message create unnecessary urgency?
Have I verified the request independently?
If something feels suspicious, stop and verify.

sections.heading: Lesson Summary
sections.body:
Cybersecurity is an essential part of professional event management.
Every employee plays a role in protecting BLKSM's information, systems and reputation.
By following secure working practices, protecting confidential information and reporting suspicious activity promptly, employees reduce organisational risk and strengthen client confidence.
Technology provides protection.
Professional behaviour provides resilience.

sections.body: Explain why cybersecurity is the responsibility of every employee rather than only the IT Department. Include examples relating to email security, password management, data protection and incident reporting. (Minimum 450 words)

lessons.reflection: Practical Exercise: Cybersecurity Risk Assessment You have been appointed as the Digital Compliance Officer for a major international conference. Prepare a cybersecurity risk assessment covering: Part 1 – Risk Identification Identify at least 15 cybersecurity risks, including: - Email attacks. - Device theft. - Weak passwords. - Data breaches. - Public Wi-Fi risks. - Social engineering. - Malware. Part 2 – Risk Mitigation For each risk recommend: - Preventive controls. - Employee responsibilities. - Technology controls. - Monitoring activities. Part 3 – Incident Response Develop a BLKSM Incident Response Checklist covering: - Detection. - Reporting. - Escalation. - Investigation. - Recovery. - Communication. - Lessons learned. Part 4 – Employee Awareness Campaign Design a one-month internal awareness campaign that includes: - Weekly security tips. - Phishing simulation. - Poster concepts. - LMS refresher training. - Monthly security quiz. Part 5 – Compliance Checklist Create a Digital Compliance Checklist for all employees covering: - Password standards. - Device security. - Document handling. - Data sharing. - Remote working. - System access. - Information classification. Submission Requirements - Length: 2,500–3,000 words. - Assessment Focus: Cybersecurity awareness, risk management, data protection, digital compliance and incident response.
